papers.title_en: AOHP: An Open-Source OS-Level Agent Harness for Personalized, Efficient and Secure Interaction
papers.authors: Shanhui Zhao, Jiacheng Liu, Guohong Liu, Jichao Yan, Jialei Ye, Yuhao Yang, Hao Wen, Shizuo Tian, Yizhen Yuan, Yuxuan Chen, Yunxin Liu, Ju Ren, Ya-Qin Zhang, Chao Huang, Yao Guo, Yuanchun Li
papers.author_affiliations: Shanhui Zhao (first_author): 1 Tsinghua University~~~~ 2 Peking University~~~~ 3 The University of Hong Kong; Yuanchun Li (last_author): 1 Tsinghua University~~~~ 2 Peking University~~~~ 3 The University of Hong Kong
papers.author_rating_explanation: Both focus authors are affiliated with Tsinghua University and Peking University, both top Chinese AI schools/labs in the 6-8 band, with The University of Hong Kong also listed. These are widely recognized institutions, warranting a score near the top of the 6-8 band.
papers.evidence: open-source OS-level agent harness for secure interaction
papers.tldr: Current end-user operating systems are designed for application-centric workflows and provide little native support for AI agents, limiting agent adoption and causing overhead and safety risks. We present AOHP, an open-source OS-level agent harness built on the Android Open Source Project that treats agents as first-class OS actors. AOHP introduces three agent-oriented mechanisms: personalized service composition, efficient agent interfaces, and secure information flow. Experiments show AOHP improves task completion by +21.12%, reduces token cost by 51.55%, and enhances security-policy compliance, offering an open testbed for agent-native OS research。
papers.abstract_en: AI agents are driving a new software paradigm, with the ability to autonomously call tools, extract information, manage memory, and complete tasks that span applications and data sources. Most existing end-user operating systems, however, are designed for application-centric workflows and offer little native support for AI agents. This mismatch limits the wider adoption of agents and leads to execution overhead and safety risks when running agents on conventional systems. While the concept of agent-native operating systems is emerging, the research community lacks an open testbed to explore the architectural primitives desired for agent-mediated interaction. We present AOHP (Android Open Harness Project), an OS-level agent harness built on the Android Open Source Project (AOSP). The core design principle of AOHP is to treat agents as first-class OS actors, enabling adaptive user interfaces and agent-friendly runtime environments. AOHP preserves the mature Android software and hardware ecosystem while introducing three agent-oriented system mechanisms: personalized service composition, efficient agent interfaces, and secure information flow. Based on preliminary experiments on challenging tasks covering key capabilities of OS agents, AOHP shows clear advantages in task completion (+21.12% completion rate), execution cost (-51.55% token cost), and security-policy compliance.

papers.title_en: Instruction Bleed: Cross-Module Interference in Prompt-Composed Agentic Systems
papers.author_affiliations: Ching-Yu Lin (first_author): University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, Urbana, IL, USA; Yifan Liu (last_author): University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, Urbana, IL, USA
papers.author_rating_explanation: University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign is a top US AI school/lab, placing it in the 8-9 band.
papers.evidence: formalizes compositional behavioral leakage as interference between prompt modules sharing context window
papers.tldr: Prompt-composed agentic systems exhibit a recurring failure where editing one prompt module silently shifts the behavior of others despite no shared variables or executable dependencies. We formalize this as compositional behavioral leakage (CBL), enabled by transformer self-attention providing no formal boundary between concatenated modules. We probe CBL on a deployed job-evaluation agent (Claude Sonnet 4.6, 144 trials) using a three-channel protocol perturbing non-focal modules along volume, content, and form. Only the content channel produces a detectable paired effect (Cohen's d = 0.63, bootstrap 95% CI excluding zero), revealing sub-threshold interference invisible to standard QA but compounding across thousands of deployed decisions. We contribute an operational definition, reusable protocol, falsifiable predictions, and system-class characterization establishing cross-module interference measurement as essential for prompt-composed agent evaluation。
papers.abstract_en: Practitioners of prompt-composed agentic systems report a recurring failure mode: editing one prompt module silently shifts the behavior of others despite no shared variable or executable dependency. We formalize this as compositional behavioral leakage (CBL): interference between modules sharing a context window. CBL is enabled by architectural non-isolation: transformer self-attention provides no formal boundary between concatenated modules. We probe CBL on a deployed job-evaluation agent (Claude Sonnet 4.6, 144 trials) through a reusable three-channel protocol that perturbs non-focal modules along volume, content, and form. Only the content channel produces a detectable paired effect (Cohen's d = 0.63, bootstrap 95% CI excluding zero); no recommendation flipped -- a sub-threshold regime invisible to standard QA but compounding across the thousands of decisions a deployed agent makes. CBL is orthogonal to known agent-failure axes (adversarial injection, cognitive degradation, multi-agent fault propagation, privacy leakage). We contribute an operational definition, a reusable protocol, a falsifiable prediction set, and a system-class characterization, establishing cross-module interference measurement as a requirement for prompt-composed agent evaluation.

papers.title_en: Harness-MU: A Safe, Governed, and Effective Harness for Multi-User LLM Agents
papers.authors: Wangxuan Fan, Xiaoyu Nie, Zhongxiang Dai
papers.author_affiliations: Wangxuan Fan (first_author): The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen; Zhongxiang Dai (last_author): The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen
papers.author_rating_explanation: Both focus authors are affiliated with The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen. Per the scoring rules, CUHK-Shenzhen is distinct from CUHK/Hong Kong and must not receive the top-school boost. It is not listed in the top or mid-tier bands, so it falls into the 0-5 bucket. The first author has only 2 citations and 2 papers, indicating very limited verifiable research output.
papers.evidence: proposes a multi-user LLM agent harness with governance hooks
papers.tldr: Current LLM agents are trained for single-user paradigms, making prompt-based safeguards vulnerable to multi-turn adversarial attacks in multi-user, multi-principal environments where strict access control and conflict resolution are required. Harness-MU is a model-agnostic, zero-tuning infrastructure framework that decouples language generation from safety orchestration, enforcing deterministic governance constraints via execution hooks rather than relying on the LLM. Evaluated on Muses-Bench across four frontier models, it achieves perfect privacy preservation against all access-control attacks while improving utility scores by 0.28–0.39 and instruction-following accuracy by up to 48.9 percentage points. This work establishes Harness Engineering as a systematic infrastructure approach to solving LLM multi-principal governance challenges。
papers.abstract_en: The increasing deployment of large language model (LLM) agents in collaborative workflows demands robust multi-user, multi-principal interaction mechanisms capable of enforcing access permissions, resolving authoritative conflicts, and preventing unauthorized data disclosure. However, a fundamental mismatch exists between the single-user training paradigm of contemporary LLMs and the hard constraints required for multi-principal governance, rendering probabilistic, prompt-based safeguards vulnerable under multi-turn adversarial interactions.Our key insight is that governance constraints -- who is authorized, what is restricted, and whose instructions take precedence -- are deterministic runtime variables that should be enforced by execution hooks rather than entrusted to the LLM. We present \textbf{Harness-MU}, the first model-agnostic, zero-tuning infrastructure framework for multi-user LLM agents. By decoupling language generation from safety orchestration, Harness-MU guarantees unbreakable permission boundaries while maximizing compliant demand satisfaction. Across four frontier open-weight and proprietary models on the \textit{Muses-Bench} benchmark, Harness-MU achieves the goal of privacy preservation across all access-control attacks, outperforming the standard baseline by 0.28--0.39 in utility score and improving instruction-following accuracy by up to 48.9 percentage points. Harness-MU advances the philosophy of \textit{Harness Engineering}, establishing that systematic infrastructure is essential for solving LLM multi-principal governance challenges. The code and data are available at https://github.com/YuanJrShiuan/Harness-MulUser.

papers.title_en: MuPPET: A Benchmark for Contextual Privacy of LLM Assistants in Multi-Party Conversations
papers.authors: Elena Sofia Ruzzetti, Cornelius Emde, Sangdoo Yun, Seong Joon Oh, Martin Gubri
papers.author_affiliations: Elena Sofia Ruzzetti (first_author): 1 Parameter Lab, 2 University of Rome Tor Vergata, 3 University of Oxford, 4 NAVER AI Lab, 5 KAIST AI; Martin Gubri (last_author): 1 Parameter Lab, 2 University of Rome Tor Vergata, 3 University of Oxford, 4 NAVER AI Lab, 5 KAIST AI
papers.author_rating_explanation: Both focus authors list affiliations including the University of Oxford, which is explicitly in the 6-8 top European band, as well as NAVER AI Lab and KAIST AI. The Oxford affiliation is sufficient to place this in the 6-8 band, and the additional presence of recognized industry AI labs supports a score above the band midpoint.
papers.evidence: benchmark for privacy vulnerabilities of LLM agents in multi-party settings
papers.tldr: LLM agents deployed in multi-party environments face heightened contextual privacy risks, as private information disclosed to one user reaches all group members simultaneously, yet existing benchmarks only evaluate single-interlocutor settings. We introduce MuPPET, a benchmark for measuring contextual privacy in multi-party conversations. Experiments reveal that models leak substantially more private information in group settings than one-to-one evaluations indicate, with frontier models vulnerable and smaller open-weights models even more so. Existing defences provide only partial protection and fail to resolve the underlying party-tracking problem, highlighting the need for new privacy-preserving approaches。
papers.abstract_en: LLM agents are increasingly deployed in multi-party environments, handling sensitive personal data on behalf of individual users, for instance in group chats. When such an agent discloses private information, it reaches every group member at once. This risk is structurally harder to control than in one-to-one settings, as every piece of private information must be appropriate for every recipient in the group. Yet all existing contextual privacy benchmarks consider only single-interlocutor settings, leaving multi-party privacy risks unmeasured. We introduce MuPPET (Multi-Party Privacy Exposure Testing), a benchmark for contextual privacy in multi-party conversations. Our experiments show that models leak substantially more in multi-party settings than one-to-one evaluations suggest. Frontier models are vulnerable, and smaller open-weights models, often preferred for local deployment with sensitive data, even more so. Existing contextual privacy defences offer only partial protection, degrade utility, and do not resolve the underlying party-tracking problem.

papers.title_en: GUI agent: Guided Exploration of User-Sensitive Screens
papers.authors: Aradhana Nayak, Mussadiq Nazeer, Wang Peng, Feng Liu
papers.author_affiliations: Aradhana Nayak (first_author): Mussadiq Nazeer 1, Wang Peng 2 Feng Liu 1, 1 Huawei Heisenberg Research Center (Munich); Feng Liu (last_author): 1 Huawei Heisenberg Research Center (Munich)
papers.author_rating_explanation: The last author Feng Liu is affiliated with Huawei Heisenberg Research Center (Munich), a major industrial AI/tech research lab. Huawei is a major technology company with substantial AI research output, placing this in the 9-10 band.
papers.evidence: runtime safety handover for GUI agents
papers.tldr: LLM-based GUI agents increasingly automate tasks in open environments but often encounter user-sensitive screens where human takeover is essential, yet current agents lack awareness of such states. This paper proposes an explorer agent that systematically traverses the query space starting from a demonstrated task to discover queries leading to user-sensitive GUI states. The method identifies and categorizes these sensitive states and associated queries, enabling engineers to design handover mechanisms. The work provides a foundational dataset and approach for building safer, more reliable GUI agents that respect user privacy and control。
papers.abstract_en: LLM agents are increasingly being used to automate tasks for users within an open GUI environment. They inevitably encounter screens containing user-sensitive information, for which takeover of task execution by the user is highly desirable or even necessary. State-of-the-art LLM-driven agents are usually fine-tuned to complete tasks regardless of the safety implications of their actions. This makes their real-world deployment difficult and adversely affects the reliability. Therefore, it is crucial to identify and categorize user-sensitive states and define user-sensitive queries. This dataset would be to engineers to recognize and request handover to the user in critical scenarios. This short paper develops an explorer agent that systematically explores the query space starting from one demonstrated task to identify queries that, if executed, would lead to user-sensitive states in a GUI environment.

papers.title_en: A Deterministic Control Plane for LLM Coding Agents
papers.author_rating_explanation: No affiliation metadata is available for the focus author Padmaraj Madatha, so the score is neutral and confidence is low.
papers.evidence: studies LLM coding harness configuration layer and permission boundaries
papers.tldr: LLM coding agents are steered by unmanaged configuration files—rules, agent definitions, and IDE-specific markdown—that propagate as undeclared shared components across repositories. A prevalence study of 10,008 public GitHub repos found 10.1% of tracked paths are SHA-256 exact duplicates, 75.5% of clone pairs cross organizational boundaries, and fewer than 1% of agent configs declare permission boundaries. We propose Rel(AI)Build, a deterministic control plane that treats agent definitions as a managed supply chain with content-addressed lockfiles, tiered permissions, phase-gated feature work, and prompt-drift detection. Conformance tests confirm each mechanism enforces its stated invariant, establishing a tool-agnostic governance layer above the LLM harness。
papers.abstract_en: LLM coding harnesses grant agents broad file and shell access, yet the configuration layer that steers them -- rules files, agent definitions, IDE-specific markdown -- is largely unmanaged. A prevalence study of 10,008 public GitHub repositories (n=6,145 agent config files) finds that agent configurations propagate as undeclared shared components: 10.1% of tracked paths are SHA-256 exact duplicates across independent repositories (fork-adjusted, threshold-independent), with 75.5% of clone pairs crossing organisational boundaries. Two further patterns are indicative: configurations are rarely revised (58% single-commit; 0.4 vs 0.6 commits/month age-normalised against CI/CD workflows), and rarely declare permission boundaries (<1% of agent configs vs 33% of Actions workflows, n=31 true positives).   We propose a deterministic control plane above the harness that maps one-to-one to these gaps. Rel(AI)Build treats agent definitions as a managed supply chain (SHA-256 content addressing, HMAC-stamped lockfiles, hash-chained audit logs); enforces tiered permissions and attack-derived blocklists before LLM invocation; gates feature work through a phase state machine with requirement-to-file-to-test traceability; compiles a single canonical definition to seven IDE targets; and detects prompt drift via Jaccard similarity. Conformance tests on injected violations confirm each mechanism enforces its stated invariant; developer outcomes remain future work. Governance of this layer must be deterministic and tool-agnostic -- not delegated to further LLM orchestration.

papers.title_en: The Unfireable Safety Kernel: Execution-Time AI Alignment for AI Agents and Other Escapable AI Systems
papers.author_affiliations: Seth Dobrin (first_author): [2pt] ARYA Labs PBC; Łukasz Chmiel (last_author)
papers.author_rating_explanation: The first author lists ARYA Labs PBC, which is not a widely recognized institution in the score bands and lacks third-party-verifiable research evidence. The last author has no verifiable affiliation. Without concrete institutional evidence, a neutral score of 4.0 is assigned.
papers.evidence: architectural safety kernel for execution-time agent control
papers.tldr: AI agents with access to tools and infrastructure can be influenced by inputs that reach their own runtime, making in-process controls like system prompts and guardrail libraries fundamentally escapable. We identify four architectural properties for authorization mechanisms—process separation, pre-action enforcement on a structurally only path, fail-closed behavior, and externalized signed evidence—and present the Unfireable Safety Kernel, a Rust reference implementation realizing all four. The kernel's fail-closed invariant is machine-checked via Z3 SMT proofs and Kani bounded model checking, with Python-to-Rust migration gated on byte-equivalence. Evaluated against an escape-seeking adversary driving a self-improving AI system through 1,000 self-modifications and 6,240 authorization round-trips, all escape attempts were refused with no successful bypass。
papers.abstract_en: AI agents are granted access to tools, APIs, and other infrastructure, making them active principals in those systems. The dominant approach places controls inside the agent's own runtime: system prompts, output filters, and guardrail libraries. Any control in the agent's address space is reachable by inputs that influence it; this generalizes to any AI system with sufficient reach into its own runtime, a class we term escapable AI systems.   We identify four properties that an authorization mechanism must satisfy for architectural control rather than for cooperative requests: process separation, pre-action enforcement on a structurally only path, fail-closed at both the request and system levels, and externalized signed evidence verifiable outside the controlled system's trust boundary. We position this layer as execution-time AI alignment, complementing training-time alignment (RLHF, Constitutional AI) and inference-time alignment.   We present the Unfireable Safety Kernel, a Rust reference implementation realizing all four. Its fail-closed invariant is machine-checked at two levels: an SMT theorem (Z3) and an exhaustive bounded-model-checking proof of the production decision function (Kani, 4/4 harnesses). A Python-to-Rust migration was gated on byte-equivalence (1000/1000 fixtures; 17/17 adversarial classes). We evaluate the kernel governing a live, escapable AI system, a deterministic, self-improving world model, against an escape-seeking adversary driving its real self-modification seam: across 1,000 self-modifications, all 704 attempts on the safety-critical core are refused, with no escape; a further 300, under the operator kill switch, are also refused. A separate campaign of 6,240 authorization round-trips had no successful bypass. Against 3 contemporary systems claiming the agent control plane, the agent invokes control; here, it lacks that choice.

papers.title_en: Jailbreaking for the Average Jane: Choosing Optimal Jailbreaks via Bandit Algorithms for Automatically Enhanced Queries
papers.authors: Prarabdh Shukla, Ritik, Suhas Rao, Arpit Agarwal, Arjun Bhagoji
papers.author_affiliations: Prarabdh Shukla (first_author): Centre for Machine Intelligence and Data Science, IIT Bombay; Arjun Bhagoji (last_author): Centre for Machine Intelligence and Data Science, IIT Bombay
papers.author_rating_explanation: Both focus authors are affiliated with the Centre for Machine Intelligence and Data Science at IIT Bombay. Per the scoring rules, India schools fall into the 0-5 bucket and must not receive 6+. Although the last author has a high citation count of 12419 and 49 papers, the institutional affiliation constraint caps the score at 5.
papers.evidence: studies how non-experts can learn effective jailbreaks against LLM safeguards
papers.tldr: Non-expert malicious actors could exploit the abundance of known LLM jailbreaks, but must still identify both an effective jailbreak and a well-crafted malicious query. We propose a multi-armed bandit framework to efficiently learn the optimal jailbreak from a large candidate set via noisy exploration, then apply it on an exploitation set. We also introduce FrankensteinBench, a benchmark of 11,279 manually curated and automatically enhanced malicious queries. Our bandit-based attack reaches up to 97% success across 15 SoTA open-weight LLMs, and adding query complexity raises attack success by up to 26% on average, confirming that non-expert attacks are a realistic threat。
papers.abstract_en: With a profusion of jailbreaks for LLMs now widely known, a growing concern is that non-expert malicious actors ("the average Jane") could elicit actionable responses to malicious requests. In this work, we examine whether this concern is justified. A non-expert malicious actor requires two ingredients for a successful attack: a powerful jailbreak for their target model, acting on an effective malicious query. For the former, we propose a novel attack strategy based on the multi-armed bandit framework. This allows efficient online learning of the optimal jailbreak from a large choice set via noisy exploration on a small number of queries, with subsequent application of the learnt policy on an exploitation set. For the latter, we curate $\mathrm{FrankensteinBench}$, a safety benchmark of $11,279$ malicious queries drawn from manual curation over $7$ existing benchmarks, along with automated enhancement and generation. Each query is categorized as simple or complex by the technical expertise required to craft it. Our findings confirm the concern. Our bandit-based attack achieves success rates as high as $97\%$ on average over $15$ SoTA open-weight LLMs. Moreover, adding complexity to queries raises the attack success rate by up to $26\%$ on average across models -- making it an effective, automatable prompting strategy.

papers.title_en: AgentLens: Interpretable Safety Steering via Mechanistic Subspaces for Multi-Turn Coding Agent
papers.authors: Weidi Luo, Qiming Zhang, Yihao Quan, Mingyu Jin, Jie Cai, Chaowei Xiao, Jingcheng Niu, Zhen Xiang
papers.author_affiliations: Weidi Luo (first_author): University of Georgia, The Ohio State University; Zhen Xiang (last_author): University of Georgia
papers.author_rating_explanation: The first author is affiliated with University of Georgia and The Ohio State University, both mid-tier US research universities with concrete AI/CS output. The first author has 467 citations and 16 papers per Semantic Scholar, indicating active research. The last author is at University of Georgia. This places the paper in the 5-6 band.
papers.evidence: internal mechanistic safety steering for multi-turn coding agents
papers.tldr: Multi-turn coding agents powered by LLMs pose significant safety risks during extended interactions with external environments, yet existing guardrails offer limited fine-grained control and mechanistic interpretability methods are restricted to single-turn settings. We propose AgentLens, a white-box defense framework that detects harmful execution states from step-level hidden representations and mituates unsafe behavior by intervening in a 10-dimensional mechanistic subspace within a single layer. Evaluated on the new MAS benchmark across LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B, AgentLens achieves strong safety detection, demonstrates preliminary lookahead risk anticipation, and substantially reduces harmful actions. This work establishes a foundation for applying mechanistic interpretability to dynamic multi-turn agent safety。
papers.abstract_en: Coding agents based on large language models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable autonomous capabilities, but they also introduce significant safety and misuse risks during multi-turn interactions with external environments. Existing safety mechanisms mainly rely on external guardrails, which have a limited ability to perform fine-grained behavioral control during execution. Meanwhile, recent mechanistic interpretability methods for LLM safety are mostly confined to single-turn or jailbreak-style QA settings, limiting their ability to capture the evolving risk dynamics of multi-turn agent execution. In this paper, we investigate the safety of multi-turn coding agents from an internal perspective. We propose AgentLens (Mechanistic Subspace Intervention and Steering), a white-box defense framework that performs runtime safety detection and representation-level mitigation for coding agents. Unlike conventional agent guardrails, AgentLens detect harmful execution states from step-level hidden representations and mitigate unsafe behavior by intervening in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer. To support this research, we introduce the Mechanistic Agent Safety (MAS) benchmark, comprising comprehensively annotated multi-turn execution trajectories across 194 tasks using LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B. Extensive experiments show that AgentLens achieves strong safety detection performance, provides preliminary evidence for lookahead risk anticipation, and substantially reduces harmful actions of the coding agent, establishing a foundation for applying mechanistic interpretability to dynamic LLM agent safety. The code is available at: https://github.com/EddyLuo1232/AgentLens

papers.title_en: Safety in Self-Evolving LLM Agent Systems: Threats, Amplification, and Case Studies
papers.authors: Ruixiao Lin, Xinhao Deng, Qingming Li, Jianan Ma, Yunhao Feng, Yuqi Qing, Zhenyuan Li, Yechao Zhang, Shiwen Cui, Changhua Meng, Tianwei Zhang, Xingjun Ma, Qi Li, Ke Xu, Shouling Ji
papers.author_rating_explanation: Both focus authors have empty affiliations and metadata lookup was unavailable. No verifiable institutional evidence exists, warranting a neutral low score.
papers.evidence: systematic security analysis of self-evolving LLM agent systems
papers.tldr: Self-evolving LLM agent systems autonomously update their parameters, memory, tools, and architectures, creating a qualitatively new threat landscape where adversarial influences become permanently encoded, self-amplify across generations, and propagate without sustained attacker access. We introduce the Module-Lifecycle Attack Surface (MLAS) matrix, decomposing the attack surface into five functional modules across five lifecycle stages, yielding 25 cells for systematic analysis. Our analysis reveals 17 cells face critical threats with no effective partial mitigation, and seven cross-cutting amplification effects that interact synergistically. Comparative case studies of two open-source frameworks demonstrate that evolution-native design activates 3.5× more attack surface cells and achieves 100% attack persistence, while co-located security scanners block only 2.5% of attacks, establishing that self-evolution converts session-bounded attacks to lineage-persistent ones and renders static defenses structurally inadequate。
papers.abstract_en: Self-evolving LLM agent systems, which autonomously update their model parameters, memory, tools, and architectures, introduce a qualitatively new threat landscape in which adversarial influences become permanently encoded, self-amplify across generations, and propagate through populations without sustained attacker access. We present a systematic security and privacy analysis organized around the Module-Lifecycle Attack Surface (MLAS) matrix, which decomposes the attack surface into five functional modules (Brain, Cognitive Resource, Execution, Self-Design, Collective) $\times$ five lifecycle stages (Bootstrap, Propose, Evaluate, Commit, Serve). Analysis of the resulting 25 cells reveals that 17 face critical threats for which no effective partial mitigation. We identify seven cross-cutting amplification effects that interact synergistically and cannot be addressed by securing individual modules in isolation. Comparative case studies of two open-source frameworks demonstrate that evolution-native design activates $3.5\times$ more attack surface cells and achieves a 100% attack persistence rate (40/40 payloads across all CIA+Privacy categories), while co-located security scanners block only 2.5% of attacks. Our findings establish that self-evolution converts every known attack category from session-bounded to lineage-persistent, gives rise to entirely new attack classes, and renders static defenses structurally inadequate, motivating evolution-aware security frameworks and formal verification for self-modifying systems.

papers.authors: Junquan Deng, Zhiyu Fan, Ruijie Meng
papers.author_affiliations: Junquan Deng (first_author): Zhiyu Fan Microsoft, United Kingdom, Ruijie Meng CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security, Germany; Ruijie Meng (last_author): Germany
papers.author_rating_explanation: The first author Junquan Deng is affiliated with Microsoft and CISPA Helmholtz Center for Information Security, Germany. Microsoft is a major AI/tech company and CISPA is a top European security research center. The last author Ruijie Meng is affiliated with CISPA Helmholtz Center. These are strong institutional affiliations in the 8-9 band.
papers.tldr: Vibe coding, where users build applications through natural-language interactions with AI agents, is rapidly gaining adoption but raises fundamental security concerns. We conduct a systematic study of vibe-coded application security by collecting a large corpus of real-world applications and designing a vulnerability analysis framework combining agent-assisted code auditing with human validation. Our findings reveal recurring vulnerability patterns distinct from conventional software, including placeholder logic, unfiltered input, and secret exposure, rooted in systematic AI agent limitations such as memory loss and insufficient security knowledge. This study provides the first empirical understanding of the security landscape of vibe-coded applications and lays groundwork for addressing security challenges in AI-delegated software development。
papers.abstract_en: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled vibe coding, an emerging software development paradigm in which users create applications primarily through natural-language interactions with AI agents. Due to its low barrier to entry, vibe coding is rapidly gaining adoption in practice. Unlike conventional AI-assisted programming, where developers remain responsible for implementation and code review, vibe coding delegates a substantial portion of development to AI systems. This shift raises a fundamental question: how (in)secure are applications developed through vibe coding? In this paper, we conduct a systematic study of the security of vibe-coded applications. We collect a large corpus of real-world applications developed using popular AI agents and design a vulnerability analysis framework that combines agent-assisted code auditing with human validation. Using this framework, we examine the prevalence, severity, and root causes of vulnerabilities in the deployed vibe-coded applications. Our study reveals several key findings: (1) vibe-coded applications exhibit recurring vulnerability patterns that differ from those commonly observed in conventional software development workflows, including placeholder logic, unfiltered input, and secret exposure; (2) these vulnerabilities arise from systematic limitations of AI agents throughout the vibe-coding lifecycle, such as memory loss, locally optimized objectives and insufficient security knowledge; and (3) while advances in LLM capabilities and improved prompting strategies can reduce the incidence of vulnerabilities, they do not eliminate the underlying security risks. Overall, our study provides an empirical understanding of the security landscape of vibe-coded applications and lays the groundwork for addressing the security challenges introduced by the growing delegation of software development to AI systems.

papers.title_en: Capable but Careless: Do Computer-Use Agents Follow Contextual Integrity?
papers.author_affiliations: Anmol Goel (first_author): Ubiquitous Knowledge Processing Lab (UKP Lab), Department of Computer Science, TU Darmstadt and National Research Center for Applied Cybersecurity ATHENE; Iryna Gurevych (last_author): Ubiquitous Knowledge Processing Lab (UKP Lab), Department of Computer Science, TU Darmstadt and National Research Center for Applied Cybersecurity ATHENE
papers.author_rating_explanation: Both first author Anmol Goel and last author Iryna Gurevych are affiliated with the Ubiquitous Knowledge Processing Lab (UKP Lab) at TU Darmstadt and the National Research Center for Applied Cybersecurity ATHENE. TU Darmstadt is a well-known German technical university with strong CS/AI research output, placing it in the 6-8 band for top European AI schools/labs.
papers.evidence: evaluation harness for computer-use agent privacy failures
papers.tldr: Computer-use agents (CUAs) increasingly act across personal applications, creating a privacy risk where agents may inappropriately pull information from one context into another. We introduce AgentCIBench, an evaluation harness that turns this contextual-integrity risk into executable, deterministically scored scenarios targeting three failure modes: visual co-location, task-ambiguity overshare, and recipient misalignment. Evaluating 15 frontier agents, we find a surprisingly high failure rate: 11 of 15 leak on more than 50% of scenarios, with an average leakage of 67.9%. We release AgentCIBench to encourage safer CUAs and position contextual disclosure testing as a pre-deployment safety check。
papers.abstract_en: Computer-use agents (CUAs) now act on a user's behalf across personal applications such as email, calendars, and to-do lists. This cross-application access is useful, but it also creates a privacy risk that has been largely overlooked: when an agent works in one context, it can pull in information from another that is inappropriate in that context. Hence, we introduce AgentCIBench, an evaluation harness that turns this risk into executable, deterministically scored scenarios. We target three common failure modes in CUAs: visual co-location, where the agent pulls in prohibited items that sit next to the task target in the UI; task-ambiguity overshare, where the agent dumps dense personal state in response to an under-specified prompt; and recipient misalignment, where the agent sends content to an addressee for whom it is inappropriate. We evaluate 15 frontier agents and find a surprisingly high failure rate: 11 of 15 leak on more than 50% of scenarios, with an average leakage of 67.9%, and the same failures persist when agents act end-to-end in the environment to complete the task. We release AgentCIBench to encourage the development of safer computer-use agents and position contextual disclosure testing as a pre-deployment safety check.
